title: Giacomo Gastaldi

infobox.value: (c. 1500, in [Villafranca Piemonte](/wiki/Villafranca_Piemonte "Villafranca Piemonte") – October 1566, in [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice")) was an Italian [cartographer](/wiki/Cartographer "Cartographer"), [astronomer](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") and [engineer](/wiki/Engineer "Engineer") of the 16th century.[[1]](#cite_note-WDL1-1)

["The Drawing of the Modern Geography of the Whole Africa"](http://www.wdl.org/en/item/6764/). [World Digital Library](/wiki/World_Digital_Library "World Digital Library"). Retrieved 16 February 2013.
Moreland, Carl & David Bannister, *Antique Maps - A Collector's Guide*, (Phaidon-Christie's, Oxford, 1983), p. 66.

Burden, Philip D, *The Mapping of North America: A list of printed maps 1511-1670*, (Raleigh, England, 1996), p. 16. See also: Nordenskiöld, Adolf Erik, *Facsimile-Atlas to the Early History of Cartography*, (Dover Publications, New York, Reprint 1973), p. 28.

Burden, Philip D. *The Mapping of North America: A list of printed maps 1511-1670*, (Raleigh, England, 1996), p. 16. See also Moreland, Carl & David Bannister, *Antique Maps - A Collector's Guide*, (Phaidon-Christie's, Oxford, 1983), p. 66.

Tooley, R.V, and Charles Bricker, *Landmarks of Mapmaking*, (Elsevier-Sequoia, Amsterdam, 1968); Adolf Erik Nordenskiöld, *Facsimile-Atlas to the Early History of Cartography*, (Dover Publications, New York, Reprint 1973), p. 40.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Italian cartographer (died 1566)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Weltkarte-ptolemaeus-ausgabe-1548_1-1200x890.jpg/250px-Weltkarte-ptolemaeus-ausgabe-1548_1-1200x890.jpg)](/wiki/File:Weltkarte-ptolemaeus-ausgabe-1548_1-1200x890.jpg)

Gastaldi's world map (1548), showing North America and Asia as one continent.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Gastaldi_Nueva_Hispania_Tabula_Nova_1548_UTA.jpg/250px-Gastaldi_Nueva_Hispania_Tabula_Nova_1548_UTA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gastaldi_Nueva_Hispania_Tabula_Nova_1548_UTA.jpg)

Gastaldi's map of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") (1548)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/1551_Venice_Gastaldi-Descriptione_de_la_Moscouia.jpg/250px-1551_Venice_Gastaldi-Descriptione_de_la_Moscouia.jpg)](/wiki/File:1551_Venice_Gastaldi-Descriptione_de_la_Moscouia.jpg)

Gastaldi's map of *[Moscovia](/wiki/Moscovia_(region) "Moscovia (region)")* (1550)

**Giacomo Gastaldi** (c. 1500, in [Villafranca Piemonte](/wiki/Villafranca_Piemonte "Villafranca Piemonte") – October 1566, in [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice")) was an Italian [cartographer](/wiki/Cartographer "Cartographer"), [astronomer](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") and [engineer](/wiki/Engineer "Engineer") of the 16th century.[[1]](#cite_note-WDL1-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giacomo_Gastaldi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

Gastaldi, sometimes referred to as Jacopo,[[2]](#cite_note-Tooley,_R.V_1968-2) or Iacobo,[[3]](#cite_note-Nordenskiöld,_Adolf_Erik_1973_p._40-3) began his career as an [engineer](/wiki/Engineer "Engineer"), serving the [Venetian Republic](/wiki/Venetian_Republic "Venetian Republic") in that capacity until the fourth decade of the sixteenth century. From about 1544 he turned his attention entirely to mapmaking, and his work represents several important turning points in cartographic development.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

According to the author Philip Burden, Gastaldi's 1548 edition of [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy")'s *Geography*, "was the most comprehensive atlas produced between [Martin Waldseemüller's](/wiki/Martin_Waldseem%C3%BCller "Martin Waldseemüller") *Geographia* of 1513, and the [Abraham Ortelius](/wiki/Abraham_Ortelius "Abraham Ortelius") *Theatrum* of 1570," because it included regional maps of the Americas.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Yet Gastaldi's detailed attention to the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") was not his only contribution to the development of map production. The Ptolemy edition of 1548 was also an innovation in that Gastaldi and his publisher reduced the size of the volume, thereby making the first 'pocket' [atlas](/wiki/Atlas "Atlas").[[3]](#cite_note-Nordenskiöld,_Adolf_Erik_1973_p._40-3) Finally, Gastaldi's work also indicated a shift in cartographical technique via its use of the copper [engraving](/wiki/Engraving "Engraving"). Prior to this period, most maps had been printed from [woodcuts](/wiki/Woodcuts "Woodcuts"); by using a copper plate rather than a woodblock to print, the engraver could render a much higher level of finesse and detail.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Gastaldi was described by one contemporary as the 'most excellent Piedmontese cosmographer'.[[2]](#cite_note-Tooley,_R.V_1968-2) As a cartographer, Gastaldi worked for various publishers, such as Nicolo Bascarini and Giovanbattista Pedrezano.[[7]](#cite_note-7) But he also occasionally accepted private commissions, for example, that from Venice's [Council of Ten](/wiki/Council_of_Ten "Council of Ten"), who invited him to [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") maps of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") on the walls of a room in the [Doge's Palace](/wiki/Doge%27s_Palace,_Venice "Doge's Palace, Venice").[[2]](#cite_note-Tooley,_R.V_1968-2)

Among his other works is the [Asiae Nova Descriptio](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Image:Asiae_Nova_Descriptio_1574.JPG "commons:Image:Asiae Nova Descriptio 1574.JPG"), [engraved](/wiki/Engraving "Engraving") in [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") in 1574.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
